Idiomatic Expressions

  • 归潜志 - Title of a historical work by Liu Qi, meaning 'Records of Returning to Obscurity.'
  • 钦定四库全书 - Title of the 'Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries,' a massive compilation of Chinese literature commissioned during the Qing dynasty.
  • 内再筑子城 - Phrase meaning 'to build an inner city again,' referring to the construction of a smaller, secondary city wall inside the main one.
  • 工役大兴 - Phrase meaning 'a large-scale mobilization of labor,' indicating significant manpower was used for the construction.
  • 朝官监役 - Phrase meaning 'officials from the imperial court supervising the labor,' suggesting a high level of government oversight.
  • 分督方面 - Phrase meaning 'dividing and supervising different areas,' indicating that the work was divided into sections with specific officials overseeing each.
  • 少不前辄杖之 - Phrase meaning 'if they fall behind, they are beaten,' indicating a strict enforcement of deadlines and performance standards.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 归潜志 - Historical work by Liu Qi, a Yuan dynasty scholar, providing insights into the political and social conditions of the Jin dynasty.
  • 钦定四库全书 - Monumental compilation of Chinese literature, commissioned by Emperor Qianlong of the Qing dynasty, including texts from various dynasties.
  • 兴定初 - Early years of the Xingding era (1216-1219), a period during the Jin dynasty.
  • 南京城 - City of Kaifeng, which was the capital of the Jin dynasty and later became known as the 'Southern Capital' (Nanjing).
  • 贞祐四年十二月 - December of the fourth year of the Zhenyou era (1216), a period during the Jin dynasty.
  • 完顔萨布 - Jin dynasty official who proposed the idea of building a new inner city.

Proper Nouns

  • 归潜志 - Records of Returning to Obscurity
  • 钦定四库全书 - Imperially Approved Complete Library of the Four Treasuries
  • 兴定初 - Early years of the Xingding era (1216-1219)
  • 南京城 - Kaifeng, Southern Capital
  • 贞祐四年十二月 - December of the fourth year of the Zhenyou era (1216)
  • 完顔萨布 - Wanyan Sabu

[109]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 南渡之後 - Refers to the period after the Southern Migration, a historical term describing the relocation of the Song dynasty court to the south after the fall of Kaifeng to the Jurchens in 1127.
  • 妖怪 - Literally 'spirits' or 'supernatural beings,' but in this context likely refers to omens or signs of misfortune.
  • 白日虎入鄭門 - A literal phrase meaning 'a tiger entered Zheng Gate during the day,' an unusual and ominous event suggesting a disruption of natural order.
  • 吏部中有狐躍出 - A fox jumped out from the Ministry of Personnel. In classical Chinese, foxes are symbolic of trickery or supernatural interference, especially in government offices.
  • 烏鵲蔽天 - Crows and magpies blocked the sky. A metaphor for a large number of birds, often associated with omens of disaster or the end of an era.
  • 亡國之兆 - Signs of a fallen state. A clear reference to the fall of a dynasty or the end of an era.
  • 迄今為丘墟瓦砾 - Up to now, it has become a wasteland of ruins and rubble. A poetic way to describe the complete destruction of a city.
  • 正大末 - Refers to the last years of the reign of Emperor Wanyan Shouxian (金宣宗) under the Jin dynasty. '正大' is the era name (年号) of the Jin dynasty.
  • 北兵入河南京城 - Northern troops entered the city of Kaifeng. Kaifeng was the capital of the Northern Song dynasty before the Southern Migration.
  • 作防守計 - Made defensive plans. A military term referring to preparations for defense.
  • 官盡毀之 - The officials destroyed it all. Implies a deliberate act of destruction, possibly to prevent the enemy from using the structures.
  • 樓亭材大者為樓橹 - The large materials from the pavilions and towers were used to build siege towers. '樓橹' refers to siege towers or fortifications used in warfare.
  • 其湖石皆凿为礮矣 - All the lake stones were chiseled into cannons. This suggests the repurposing of decorative stones into military equipment.
  • 迄今皆废区壊址 - Up to now, they are all abandoned areas and ruined sites. A poetic way to describe the desolation.
  • 熙春一阁耳 - Only the Xichun Pavilion remains. '耳' is a particle used to indicate limitation or exclusivity.
  • 上下无土泥 - There was no earth or mud in the upper and lower parts. This suggests that the structure was built with minimal use of mortar or earth, possibly making it more susceptible to damage or destruction.
  • 世岂复有此良匠也 - The world no longer has such skilled artisans. A lament over the loss of craftsmanship or the decline of architectural skill.
  • 宣宗喜刑法政尚威严 - Emperor Xuanzong favored legal punishment and valued severity. '宣宗' refers to Emperor Xuanzong of Jin (金宣宗), and '刑法政' is a compound term referring to legal and penal policies.
  • 南渡之在位者多苛刻 - Those who held office during the Southern Migration were mostly harsh. Refers to the harsh governance of officials after the Song dynasty moved south.
  • 图克坦右丞思忠 - A title and name: 'Tuokedan Right Chancellor Sizhong.' '图克坦' (Tuokedan) is a clan name, likely of Jurchen origin. '右丞' is the title 'Right Chancellor' or 'Right Minister.'
  • 好用麻椎击人 - Liked to hit people with a hemp club. '麻椎' is a type of club or stick made from hemp or fiber, used as a weapon or instrument of punishment.
  • 号麻椎相公 - Known as the Hemp Club Chancellor. '号' is a nickname or title given to someone.
  • 李运使特立友之 - Li Yunshi TeLi was his friend. '运使' is a title, possibly 'Transport Commissioner' or 'Commissioner of Transport.'
  • 号半截剑 - Known as Half-Sword. A nickname, likely referring to a style of sword or a person who was feared or known for a sharp, decisive nature.
  • 冯内翰璧叔献号马刘子 - Feng Neihan Bishu Xian was called Ma Liu Zi. '内翰' is an honorific title for a literary official or Hanlin scholar.
  • 雷希顔为御史至蔡州得奸豪杖杀五百人 - Lei Xiyang, as a censor, went to Caizhou and beat to death 500 corrupt officials and villains. '杖杀' is a form of corporal punishment where the person is beaten to death with a rod.
  • 号雷半千 - Known as Lei Banqian. A nickname, possibly referring to his severity or the number of people he punished.
  • 完顔玛嘉楚富察约尔珠 - Names of individuals: 'Wanyan Majia Chu,' 'Fuchua Yuerzhu.' These are likely Jurchen or Manchu names, with clan names (完顔 for Wanyan, 富察 for Fuchua).
  • 皆以酷闻 - All were known for their cruelty. A common phrase used to describe harsh or tyrannical officials.
  • 富察和卓 - Fuchua Hezhuo. Another name, possibly a relative or associate of the previous individuals.
  • 王阿里李涣之徒胥吏中尤狡刻者也 - Wang Ali, Li Huan, and others were especially cunning and harsh among the scribes and officials. '胥吏' refers to low-ranking clerks or officials.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 南渡 - The Southern Migration of the Song dynasty in 1127, after the fall of Kaifeng to the Jurchens. This is a major historical event in Chinese history, marking the beginning of the Southern Song dynasty.
  • 南京 - In this context, 'Nanjing' likely refers to the former capital of the Southern Song dynasty, which was moved to Lin'an (modern Hangzhou), but the term '南京' may also be used here in a more general or symbolic sense.
  • 同乐园 - A former imperial garden or palace. '同乐园' may have been a name for a specific park or palace, possibly a former imperial garden of the Song dynasty.
  • 故宋龙徳宫 - The former Song dynasty Longde Palace. Longde Palace was a palace of the Song dynasty, possibly in Kaifeng or Lin'an.
  • 徽宗所修 - Built by Emperor Huizong. Emperor Huizong of Song was known for his patronage of the arts and construction projects, including gardens and palaces.
  • 正大末 - The last years of the reign of Emperor Wanyan Shouxian (金宣宗), who ruled the Jin dynasty during the late 12th and early 13th centuries.
  • 北兵 - Northern troops refers to the Jurchen forces of the Jin dynasty, who were the enemies of the Song dynasty.
  • 河南京城 - Kaifeng, the capital of the Northern Song. Kaifeng was the capital of the Northern Song before the Southern Migration.
  • 宣宗 - Emperor Xuanzong of Jin, who ruled from 1213 to 1224. He was known for his harsh policies and reliance on cruel officials.
  • 图克坦右丞思忠 - A Jurchen official of the Jin dynasty. The name '图克坦' (Tuokedan) is a clan name, and '右丞' is the title 'Right Chancellor' or 'Right Minister.'
  • 麻椎 - A club or weapon made of hemp or fiber. In this context, it is used as a symbol of cruelty and punishment.
  • 御史 - A censor or official in the imperial administration responsible for overseeing government officials and reporting misconduct.
  • 蔡州 - A city in present-day Henan province. It was a significant location during the Jin and Song dynasties.
  • 杖杀 - A form of corporal punishment in which the person is beaten to death with a rod. This was a common but brutal form of execution in imperial China.
  • 完顔 - A Jurchen clan name, often associated with the ruling class of the Jin dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 宣宗后妃皆出微贱 - Indicates that the concubines and consorts of Emperor Xuanzong were of low social status.

  • 成国夫人 - The title of a consort of Emperor Xuanzong, indicating her rank and status.

  • 自在夫人 - Another title or nickname for the consort, suggesting a life of ease and luxury.
  • 平章政事 - A high-ranking official position, equivalent to a chancellor or prime minister.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 败亡 - Means 'collapse' or 'ruin.'
  • 临事往往不肯分明可否 - Means 'often not clearly stating whether something is right or wrong when faced with matters.'

  • 互推让 - Means 'mutually shying away' or 'shying away from responsibility.'
  • 号养相体吁相体果 - This phrase is ambiguous and requires further research. It might mean 'claiming to care for each other's well-being but actually not doing so.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 南渡之后 - Refers to the period after the Southern Migration, a significant historical event in Chinese history.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 恐生事故正人君子多不得用虽用亦未久遽退闲 - Compound sentence linking the fear of incidents with the quick dismissal of virtuous officials.
  • 台谏官如陈司谏规许司谏古程雷御史皆不能终其任也 - List of officials who were unable to complete their duties, with the structure being somewhat elliptical.
  • 南渡之后近侍之权尤重 - Introduces the period after the Southern Migration and the increased power of those close to the emperor.
  • 宣宗喜用其人为耳目以伺察百官故使其奉御辈采访民间号行路御史 - Explains the actions of Emperor Xuanzong, linking his preference for certain officials with their role as 'road inspectors'.
  • 得一二事即入奏之上因以责台官漏泄皆抵罪 - Describes the reporting process and the subsequent punishment of censors for leaking information.
  • 方面之柄虽委将帅又差一奉御在军中号监战每临机制变多为所牵制辄遇敌先奔故其军多防败 - Complex statement about the power over regional administration, the role of military commanders, and the constraints placed on them.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宰执 - Refers to high-ranking officials in the government, often involved in decision-making and administration.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 南渡后屡兴师伐宋 - Historical reference to the Jin dynasty’s military actions after the fall of the Northern Song and the establishment of the Southern Song.
  • 将取地南中 - Refers to the Jin’s intention to take land in the south, particularly the territory of the Southern Song.
  • 已所有不能保而夺人所有岂有是理 - Rhetorical question expressing disbelief at the logic of seizing others’ possessions when one cannot protect their own.
  • 虽破蕲黄杀虏良多 - Refers to the Jin capturing Qizhou and Huangzhou and killing many Song soldiers, with '虏' used for enemy soldiers.
  • 较其士马物故且屡为水陷溺亦相当也 - Balanced assessment of losses on both sides, including soldiers, horses, and drowning due to water.
  • 最后盱眙军改为镇淮府以军戍之费粮数万未防亦弃云 - Complex sentence describing a military reorganization and its failure, including the cost in grain and eventual abandonment.
  • 又师还乗夏多刈熟麦以归助军储 - Describes the use of summer harvests for military logistics after the army returned.
  • 故宋人边檄有云暴卒鸱张率作如林之旅饥氓乌合驱归得罪之人 - Poetic and metaphorical description of the Jin army, to be rendered carefully to preserve its meaning.
  • 嗟乎避强欺弱望其复振难哉 - Concluding lamentation on the Jin dynasty’s decline, expressing the difficulty of revival through avoidance of strength and bullying the weak.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 甚哉风俗之移人也 - Rhetorical exclamation meaning 'How powerful is the influence of customs on people!' Sets a reflective tone.
  • 南渡后吏权大盛 - Literal meaning 'After the southward crossing, the power of officials greatly increased.' Indicates a significant shift in political power.
  • 迁转与进士等甚者反疾焉 - Means 'their promotions were equal to those of Jinshi (imperial scholars), and in some cases, even faster.' Highlights rapid advancement of officials.
  • 士大夫家有子弟读书往往不终辄令改试台部令史 - Means 'noble families often made their sons stop reading and instead sent them to take exams for administrative positions.' Reflects a shift in educational priorities.
  • 其趋进举止全学吏曹 - Means 'their behavior and mannerisms were entirely modeled after bureaucrats.' Suggests complete adoption of bureaucratic norms.
  • 舞文纳赂甚于吏辈者 - Means 'those who manipulate legal documents and accept bribes even more than bureaucrats.' Highlights the corruption in the system.
  • 惟侥幸一时进用不顾平日源流 - Means 'only hoping for temporary advancement without considering their past origins.' Criticizes the short-sightedness of those seeking power.
  • 金朝取士止以词赋经义 - Means 'the Jin dynasty selected officials solely based on poetry, prose, and Confucian classics.' Indicates a narrow focus in the selection process.
  • 不问其人材何如 - Means 'without considering the quality of the person.' Criticizes the superficial nature of the selection criteria.
  • 不识枇杷子 - Means 'did not recognize the loquat fruit.' Metaphor for ignorance or lack of knowledge.
  • 能吟喜欲狂 - Means 'could recite poetry with great enthusiasm.' Metaphor for superficial talent or showmanship.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 章宗 - Emperor Zhangzong of Jin, who reigned from 1189 to 1208.

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 興定初 - Refers to the early years of the Xingding era (1217-1224), a period in the Jin Dynasty.

  • 始诏内外七品以上官保举 - Means 'the court then issued an edict requiring officials of the seventh rank or higher to recommend candidates,' introducing a new system.

  • 资未及者借注人 - Means 'those who did not meet the qualifications were temporarily appointed,' suggesting a pragmatic approach to filling positions.
  • 一时能吏如王登庸令洛阳 - Means 'at that time, capable officials like Wang Dengyong served as magistrates in Luoyang,' highlighting specific examples of successful officials.
  • 程震威卿令陈留 - Means 'Cheng Zhenwei served as magistrate in Chenliu,' another example of a capable official.
  • 皆有治绩 - Means 'all had good governance records,' indicating the success of these officials.
  • 或入为监察御史台部官 - Means 'some were promoted to positions in the Censorate or the Six Ministries,' showing the rewards for good performance.
  • 自是居官者争以能相尚 - Means 'from then on, officials competed to be competent,' indicating a shift in the culture of governance.
  • 民亦多受赐 - Means 'the people also benefited greatly,' emphasizing the positive impact on the populace.

  • 故疲懦贪者亦多 - Means 'so there were also many weak, lazy, and greedy officials,' highlighting the negative consequences.
  • 然士大夫为之者犹自力 - Means 'however, those who were scholars and officials still exerted themselves,' showing a continued commitment among the elite.
  • 此良法也哉 - Means 'this was indeed a good law,' summarizing the initial success of the system.
  • 正大初末帝鋭于政 - Means 'in the early years of the Zhengda era, the last emperor was keen on politics,' indicating a renewed focus on governance.
  • 朝议置益政院官 - Means 'the court discussed setting up officials in the Office of Beneficial Politics,' introducing a new institution.
  • 院居宫中 - Means 'the office was located in the palace,' indicating its central role.
  • 选一时宿望有学者如杨学士云翼史修撰公燮吕待制造数人兼之 - Means 'officials from the most respected and learned scholars, such as Yang Xueshi, Yun Yi, Shi Xiu, Gong Xie, and Lu Daimao, were selected to serve in the office,' highlighting the caliber of the officials.
  • 轮直每日朝罢侍上讲尚书贞观政要数篇 - Means 'they took turns to serve daily after court sessions, advising the emperor on the *Shangshu Zhen Guan Zhengyao* (Essential Points of Governance from the Zhenguan Era) and other texts,' indicating their role in advising the emperor.
  • 间亦及民间事有补益 - Means 'occasionally they also discussed matters of the people that were beneficial,' showing their concern for the populace.
  • 杨公又与赵学士秉文共集自古治术分门号君臣政要为一编进之 - Means 'Yang Xueshi also compiled with Zhao Xueshi a collection of political strategies from ancient times, titled *Jun Chen Zhengyao* (Essential Points of Ruler and Minister), and presented it to the emperor,' indicating their contribution to political discourse.
  • 然嵗余亦罢 - Means 'however, after a year, it was abolished,' indicating the eventual failure of the initiative.

  • 望其所用得人闻见不塞者未之有也 - Phrase meaning 'those who hope to use capable people and have unobstructed knowledge have never existed,' indicating a lack of openness and capability.
  • 士大夫为吏者当以至公无心处之事自理民自服 - Phrase meaning 'officials should handle affairs with utmost fairness and impartiality, and the people will naturally submit,' emphasizing the importance of fairness.
  • 不可委曲要誉以枉义也 - Phrase meaning 'one should not twist things to gain praise at the expense of justice,' highlighting the importance of integrity.
  • 辟举为令者往往妄用心 - Phrase meaning 'those appointed as magistrates often act recklessly,' indicating a lack of proper judgment.
  • 富家与贫家讼必直贫民势家与百姓争必直百姓不问埋何如也 - Phrase meaning 'when a wealthy family sues a poor family, the poor are always right; when a powerful family sues a commoner, the commoner is always right; the commoner does not care about the outcome,' highlighting bias and injustice.
  • 又或故旧同道之家有科征必先督促不少贷至加之刑罚 - Phrase meaning 'or when there are old friends and fellow travelers, they always demand payment first and do not hesitate to impose penalties,' indicating a lack of leniency.
  • 其意以为如此示吾无私且贾细民称誉嗟乎贫富相争自有曲直彼贫民中亦有 - Phrase meaning 'they think this shows they are impartial and gain praise from the common people, but alas, in disputes between rich and poor, there is always right and wrong, and among the poor, there are also distinctions,' highlighting the complexity of social dynamics.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence is complex and requires careful parsing. It suggests that the previous administration only used clerks and seems to imply that there were no scholars (jinshi) before the Da Ding period, which is a lack of detail.
  • This sequence describes a career progression but is somewhat elliptical, requiring context to fully understand the steps.
  • This sentence is also complex, indicating that one examination led to a specific position and another led to another, but it is not clear if all followed this pattern.

  • 天防 - A reign title of Emperor Gaozong of Tang.
  • 天眷 - A reign title of Emperor Shizong of Liao.
  • 皇统 - A reign title of Emperor Shizong of Jin.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 受赇如良家子女犯奸也 - Simile comparing accepting bribes to a virtuous family's daughter committing adultery, highlighting the severity of the act.
  • 胥吏公亷如娼女守节也 - Simile comparing the integrity of clerks to a courtesan observing chastity, emphasizing their unexpected virtue.
  • 議者皆以為當屏山嘗為余言之 - Statement indicating that scholars believe the matter should be discussed, with a personal anecdote from the author.
  • 惜乎以胥吏待天下士也 - Expression of regret that clerks are treated as if they were common officials, implying a lack of respect for scholars.
  • 士大夫有氣概者往往不就 - Phrase describing how scholars with bold spirit often refuse to take office.
  • 故士大夫有氣概者往往不就 - Repeated for emphasis, reinforcing the idea that scholars with a strong character often decline positions.
  • 如雷翰林希顔魏翰林彦宋翰林飛卿及余先子 - Lists of notable scholars who either declined or briefly held office.
  • 或召補不願或暫為遽告出皆不能終其任也 - Describes various reasons why these scholars did not complete their terms.
  • 李丈欽止為余言宋制省曹有檢正皆士大夫 - Statement about a respected figure explaining the Song dynasty's system of governance.
  • 其堂吏主行移文書也 - Describes the role of clerks in handling official documents.
  • 且問余以宋制與金制孰優余以為宋制善 - Dialogue where the author is asked to compare the governance systems of the Song and Jin dynasties, and the author's response.
  • 金朝用人大概由省令史左右司郎中贠外郎首領官取其簿書精幹也 - Explains the Jin dynasty's method of selecting officials based on their administrative skills.
  • 由左右首領官選宰相執政取其奏對詳敏也 - Describes the selection process for high-ranking officials in the Jin dynasty.
  • 其經濟大略安在哉此所以在位者多長於吏事也 - Questions the overall effectiveness of the Jin dynasty's governance and suggests that officials were skilled in administrative matters but lacked broader economic vision.

  • 受赇 - Accepting bribes, a serious offense in traditional Chinese governance.
  • 良家子女犯奸 - A virtuous family's daughter committing adultery, a severe moral transgression.
  • 胥吏 - Clerks or low-ranking officials.
  • 公亷 - Integrity or honesty.
  • 娼女守節 - A courtesan observing chastity, an unusual and unexpected behavior.

  • 雷翰林希顔魏翰林彦宋翰林飛卿 - Notable scholars from the Song dynasty.

  • 李丈欽止 - A respected figure who provided information about the Song dynasty.

  • 左右司郎中贠外郎 - High-ranking officials in the government.

  • 簿書精幹 - Administrative skills.
  • 奏對詳敏 - Detailed and prompt responses to imperial inquiries.
  • 經濟大略 - Overall economic strategy.
  • 吏事 - Administrative matters.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 金朝兵制最弊 - The military system of the Jin Dynasty was most flawed.
  • 每有征伐或邉衅动 - Every time there was a campaign or border conflict.
  • 下令签军 - An order to conscript soldiers.
  • 州县骚动 - The counties and towns were agitated.
  • 家有数丁男好身手成时尽拣取无遗 - When a family had several strong men, they were all chosen without leaving any behind.
  • 号泣怨嗟 - Crying and lamenting.
  • 国家以为苦 - The state considered it a burden.
  • 驱此辈战欲其克胜艰哉 - Forcing these people to fight, hoping they would win, was difficult.
  • 贞祐初 - The early years of the Zhenyou era (1213-1217) in the Jin Dynasty.
  • 防一时任子为监官者 - Those who were temporarily appointed as supervisors.
  • 以春赴吏部调数 - To go to the Ministry of Personnel in spring for adjustments.
  • 宰执使尽防取号监官军 - The chief officials ordered them to be fully conscripted as supervisor soldiers.
  • 其人愤愠号叫交愬于台省 - These people were angry, shouted, and complained to the central government.
  • 冲宰相卤簿告丞相布萨齐勤 - They rushed through the prime minister's entourage to report to Chancellor Pu Sa Qi Qin.
  • 大怒趣左右取弓矢射去 - He became very angry and ordered his attendants to shoot arrows at them.
  • 已而上知其不可用免之 - Later, the emperor realized they were not useful and dismissed them.
  • 元光末 - The late years of the Yuanguang era (1232-1234) in the Jin Dynasty.
  • 备潼闗黄河 - Preparing for the defense of Tongguan and the Yellow River.
  • 诸使者厯郡邑 - Various envoys visited the counties and towns.
  • 自见居官者外无文武大小执事官皆拣之 - Except those who were already in office, all officials, whether civil or military, were conscripted.
  • 至许州前戸部郎中侍御史刘元规年防六十亦中选为千戸 - In Xuzhou, the former Minister of Finance and Censor Liu Yuan Gui, who was sixty years old, was also selected as a thousand-household commander.
  • 至陈州予先子以前监察御史亦为千户 - In Chenzhou, my father, who was previously an Inspector, was also made a thousand-household commander.
  • 既立部曲须依军例以次相钤束 - Once the units were established, they had to follow military regulations and be controlled in order.
  • 物议喧然后亦罢之 - After much public criticism, it was eventually abolished.
  • 嗟乎以任子为兵已失体况以朝士大夫充斯役乎 - Alas, conscripting officials as soldiers has already lost its dignity, let alone conscripting court officials.
  • 当是时余以终塲举人获免而先子以御史不免 - At that time, I was exempted as a final candidate, but my father, who was an Inspector, was not.
  • 立法之以至于斯 - The flaws in the law led to this.
  • 余赴试开封先子以诗送之且寄赵闲闲雷希顔有云老作一兵吾命也芳聮七桂汝身之厚禄故人如见问为言尘土困王尼二公览之一笑 - I went to Kaifeng for the exam, and my father sent me a poem to accompany me, also sending it to Zhao Xianxian and Lei Xiyang, saying 'Old man, I am a soldier, it is my fate. You have a thick salary, seven good connections, and if old friends ask, tell them I am trapped in the dust like Wang Ni.' The two gentlemen read it and smiled.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 金朝 - Refers to the Jin Dynasty, a Chinese dynasty that ruled northern China from 1115 to 1234.
  • 贞祐 - Refers to the Zhenyou era (1213-1217) in the Jin Dynasty.
  • 元光 - Refers to the Yuanguang era (1232-1234) in the Jin Dynasty.
  • 潼闗 - Refers to Tongguan, a strategic pass in Shaanxi Province.
  • 黄河 - Refers to the Yellow River, one of the major rivers in China.
  • 许州 - Refers to Xuzhou, a city in Henan Province.
  • 陈州 - Refers to Chenzhou, a city in Henan Province.
  • 开封 - Refers to Kaifeng, the capital of Henan Province during the Jin Dynasty.
  • 赵闲闲 - Refers to Zhao Xianxian, a person mentioned in the text.
  • 雷希顔 - Refers to Lei Xiyang, a person mentioned in the text.
  • 王尼 - Refers to Wang Ni, a person mentioned in the text.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 近習之權甚重 - The power of the close attendants is very great.
  • 職雖五品其要密與宰相等 - Though their rank is fifth grade, their importance is as close as that of the prime minister.
  • 要密 - Important and confidential or crucial and secret.
  • 如舊日中書 - Like the old Ministry of the Secretariat (Zhongshu).
  • 多以資戚世家恩幸者居其職 - Most of those who hold this position are from aristocratic families, favored by imperial grace.
  • 士大夫不預焉 - Scholars and officials are not included in this.
  • 案金史百官志 - According to the Jin Shi (History of the Jin Dynasty), Bai Guan Zhi (Treatise on Officials).
  • 提防正五品 - The head (or chief) of the bureau is of the fifth grade.
  • 使從五品副使從六品直長正八品 - The deputy head is sixth grade, the assistant is seventh grade, and the director is eighth grade.
  • 此志云職五品 - This record says the position is fifth grade.
  • 除授局職多以優等之官 - Appointments to the bureau are often given to officials of superior rank.
  • 以正七品之台官轉正八品之直長 - An official of the seventh grade from the Censorate was promoted to the eighth grade position of director.
  • 仍云擢則其體特優于常秩可知 - Still, it is called a promotion, which shows that the position is particularly superior to the normal rank.
  • 兼此職者亦多用優等之官 - Those who hold this position alongside others are also often given to superior officials.
  • 南渡后人主尤委任 - After the southern relocation, the emperor especially entrusted this position.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 金朝 - Jin Dynasty (1115–1234), a dynasty ruled by the Jurchen people.
  • 近侍局 - Jinshi Ju (Bureau of Attendants), an imperial administrative bureau responsible for managing the emperor’s close attendants and personal staff.
  • 宰相 - Prime Minister or Counselor to the Emperor, the highest-ranking officials in the government.
  • 中書 - Zhongshu Sheng (Ministry of the Secretariat), a central government department responsible for drafting and issuing imperial edicts.
  • 士大夫 - Scholars and officials, the elite intellectual and bureaucratic class in traditional China.
  • 监察御史 - Inspector of the Censorate, an official in the Yushi Tai (Imperial Censorate), responsible for oversight and reporting corruption.
  • 贞祜 - Zhenhu (1214–1217), the reign title of Emperor Xizong of Jin.
  • 武卫军副都指挥使 - Deputy Commander of the Wuwei Army, a military title.
  • 提防近侍局 - Chief of the Bureau of Attendants, a high-ranking position.
  • 秘书监 - Director of the Imperial Library, an official responsible for managing the imperial library and archives.
  • 签书枢密院事 - Deputy Director of the Military Council, a high-ranking military official.
  • 南渡 - Southern relocation, referring to the move of the Jin court from its northern base to the south after the fall of Zhongdu (modern Beijing).

[127]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 大抵視宰執台部官皆外人 - General statement meaning 'Generally speaking, the prime ministers and officials of the ministries and departments are all outsiders.' The character  is likely a variant or corrupted form of '而' (while) or '乃' (but), though its exact meaning is uncertain. It may indicate contrast or negation.
  • 所谓腹心则此局也 - 'So-called inner circle is this office.' This is a classical way of introducing a key institution or group.
  • 其局官以下所谓奉御奉职辈 - 'The officials below the head of this office, so-called 'Fengyu' and 'Fengzhi' ranks.' These are likely ranks or titles within the office.
  • 人主委信反在士大夫右 - 'The emperor places trust in them more than in the scholar-officials.' The phrase '右' here is used metaphorically to mean 'more than' or 'above.'
  • 或被命出外帅臣郡守百计馆馈 - 'Sometimes they are ordered to go out and the military governors and prefects go to great lengths to entertain them.' This shows the high status of these officials.
  • 此曹皆膏粱子弟 - 'These people are all sons of the wealthy and well-born.' '膏粱' refers to the rich and noble.
  • 惟以妆饰体様相夸 - 'They only boast about their appearance and physique.' This reflects the superficial values of the group.
  • 膏面镊须鞌马衣服鲜整 - 'They groom their faces, trim their beards, ride fine horses, and wear neat clothes.' This describes their luxurious and ostentatious lifestyle.
  • 朝夕侍上迎合谄媚 - 'They serve the emperor day and night, flattering and flattery.' This shows their role as sycophants.
  • 以逸乐尊人主安其身 - 'They provide leisure and pleasure to the emperor, securing their own position.' This is a cynical view of their role.
  • 沮壊正人招贿赂为不法 - 'They undermine the virtuous, accept bribes, and act illegally.' This is a criticism of their corrupt behavior.
  • 至于大臣退黜百官得罪多自局中 - 'As for the dismissal of high officials and the punishment of officials, it often comes from within this office.' This indicates the power of the office.
  • 御史之权反在其下矣 - 'The authority of the censors is beneath them.' This is a clear statement of the office's dominance.
  • 又于台部令史选奉职数人 - 'They also selected several officials from the commandery and department clerks.' This refers to a recruitment process.
  • 又于进士中亦选一二人充备 - 'They also selected one or two from the jinshi (imperial examination graduates) to fill the ranks.' This shows an attempt to bring in more educated individuals.
  • 其人既入局中则趋进举止曽亦未闻有正言补益者 - 'Once these people entered the office, their conduct and behavior were never heard to have any upright words or beneficial contributions.' This is a critique of their behavior.
  • 且此曹本仆役之职士大夫处之可羞而一二子泰然自以为荣亦陋也 - 'Moreover, these people were originally servants; it is shameful for scholar-officials to be in their position, yet a few of them regard it as an honor, which is truly vulgar.' This is a moral judgment on the corruption of values.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宰执台部官 - Refers to high-ranking officials such as prime ministers, ministers of the imperial court, and officials of the central ministries. These were key figures in the imperial bureaucracy.
  • 奉御奉职 - These are likely titles or ranks within the imperial service, possibly related to the palace or the imperial household. 'Fengyu' and 'Fengzhi' may be specific ranks or roles.
  • 人主 - Refers to the emperor or sovereign.
  • 士大夫 - A class of scholar-officials, the intellectual and administrative elite of the state.
  • 帅臣郡守 - Refers to military governors and prefects, local officials with both civil and military authority.
  • 膏粱子弟 - A term for the sons of wealthy and noble families, often associated with idleness and superficiality.
  • 妆饰体様 - Refers to physical appearance and grooming, a common concern in classical Chinese society.
  • 御史 - Refers to censors or officials responsible for monitoring the conduct of other officials. This was an important check on power.
  • 台部 - Likely refers to the Ministry of Justice or the office of the censors, which oversaw corruption and official misconduct.
  • 进士 - Refers to individuals who passed the highest level of the imperial examination, the jinshi degree, and were eligible for official positions.
  • 台部令史 - Refers to clerks or low-level officials in the Ministry or the Censorate.

  • 人主 - A general term for the emperor or sovereign.
  • 士大夫 - A class of scholar-officials.
  • 帅臣郡守 - Refers to military governors and prefects.

  • 御史 - Refers to censors or officials responsible for monitoring the conduct of other officials.

  • 台部令史 - Refers to clerks or low-level officials in the Ministry or the Censorate.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 金朝 - Refers to the Jin Dynasty, a Chinese dynasty that ruled northern China from 1115 to 1234.
  • 词赋 - Refers to classical Chinese poetry and prose, which were highly valued in the Jin Dynasty.
  • 律赋 - A specific form of classical Chinese poetry that was emphasized in the Jin Dynasty.
  • 南渡 - Refers to the Southern Migration, a historical event where the Song Dynasty moved its capital southward after the fall of the Northern Song to the Jurchen people.
  • 古学 - Refers to the study of ancient texts and traditions, which became more prominent after the Southern Migration.
  • 名士 - Refers to distinguished scholars or intellectuals who were known for their literary and scholarly achievements.
  • 科举 - Refers to the imperial examination system, which was used to select government officials in China.
  • 四篇文字 - Refers to the four types of essays required in the imperial examinations: the policy essay, the poetry essay, the prose essay, and the essay on current affairs.
  • 制诰之才 - Refers to the ability to write imperial edicts and official documents, which was highly valued in the imperial court.
  • 风骚之防 - Refers to the ability to write poetry in the style of the *Book of Songs* and *Chu Ci*, which were considered the highest forms of Chinese poetry.
  • 策 - Refers to the policy essay, which required candidates to propose solutions to current political issues.

Proper Nouns

  • 刘祁 - The author of the text, a scholar and writer from the Jin Dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 经济之业论以攷识鉴之方 - Discussing the economic professions to examine the methods of identification and evaluation.
  • 四者俱工其人才为何如也 - If all four aspects are skilled, what kind of talent is it?
  • 学者不知狃于习俗止力为律赋 - Scholars do not realize they are limited by custom, focusing only on composing regulated verse.
  • 至于诗策论俱不留心其基于为有司者止考赋而不究诗策论也 - As for poetry, policy essays, and arguments, they are not given attention; the fault lies with the examiners who only test composition and do not delve into poetry, policy essays, and arguments.
  • 吾尝记故老云泰和间有司攷诗赋已定去取及读策论则止用笔防庙讳御名且数字数与涂注之多寡有司如此欲举子辈専精难矣 - I once heard from old scholars that during the Tahe period, examiners decided on the selection of poetry and composition, and when reading policy essays and arguments, they only used their pens to avoid temple taboos and imperial names, and the number of characters and annotations varied. With examiners like this, it is difficult for candidates to specialize.
  • 南渡后赵杨诸公为有司方于策论中取人故士风稍变颇加意策论又于诗赋中亦辨别读书人才以是文风稍振然亦谤议纷纭然每贡举非数公为有司则又如旧矣 - After the Southern Crossing, officials like Zhao and Yang selected candidates based on policy essays and arguments, so the scholarly风气changed somewhat, and more attention was paid to policy essays and arguments. They also distinguished between reading talents in poetry and composition, thus the literary风气was somewhat revitalized, but there were also many criticisms. However, every time the candidates were selected, if these few officials were not in charge, it would be like the old days again.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 皓松漠纪闻 - A historical or biographical record, possibly a family chronicle.
  • 金制乡试 - The Jin Dynasty's system of local-level imperial examinations.
  • 本县令为试官 - Means 'the county magistrate serves as the examiner,' indicating the role of the local official in the exam.
  • 乡元 - Refers to the top scorer in the local exam.
  • 府试分三路 - Means 'the prefectural exam is divided into three routes,' suggesting a regional division in the exam system.
  • 府元 - Refers to the top scorer in the prefectural exam.
  • 会试集诸路举人于燕京 - Means 'the metropolitan exam gathers candidates from all regions in Yanjing (Beijing),' indicating the centralization of the exam in the capital.
  • 敕头 - Another term for the top scorer in the metropolitan exam, often called '状元' (Zhuangyuan).
  • 状元 - The highest-ranking scholar in the imperial examination system.
  • 四元 - Refers to the top scorers in the four levels of the exam system: local, prefectural, metropolitan, and palace exams.
  • 殿试 - The final level of the imperial examination, conducted by the emperor.
  • 孟宗献中大定三年进士 - Means 'Meng Zongxian passed the imperial examination in the third year of the Dading period (1163),' indicating a specific historical event.
  • 吾祖孟师 - Refers to the author's ancestor, Meng Zongxian, who is respected as a teacher.
  • 虚静居 - A title or pseudonym, possibly referring to a place or state of mind.
  • 恬淡 - Means 'quiet and unworldly,' describing a calm and detached attitude.
  • 留意养生术 - Means 'paying attention to methods of longevity,' indicating an interest in health and longevity practices.
  • 金丹赋 - A literary work, possibly a poem or essay on alchemy or longevity.
  • 诗词亦有集 - Means 'his poetry and verse also have collections,' indicating that Meng Zongxian's works were compiled.

  • 硬语 - Describes stiff or rigid language, often used to criticize overly formal or unnatural writing.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The difficulty of writing regulated verse and the need for skillful craftsmanship are introduced in the sentence '律诗最难工，须要工巧周圆.'
  • The opinion of Dang Gong, emphasizing the importance of each word in a poem, is described in the sentence '吾闻竹溪党公论以为五十六字皆如圣贤中有一字不经罏锤便若一屠沽子厠其间也.'
  • The difficulty of making each line striking and the importance of a key couplet in a poem are discussed in the sentence '又云八句皆要警抜极难一篇中须要一联好句为主后但以意收拾之足为好诗矣.'
  • A discussion of a poem and its subtle meaning is presented in the sentence '又尝与余论诗曰选诗曰南登灞陵岸回首望长安朔风动秋草边马有归心明月照高楼流光正徘徊此其含蓄意几何.'
  • The value of short poems is contrasted with the tendency of modern poets to write in a stiff manner in the sentence '小诗贵风骚今人往往止作硬语非也.'

  • 山河残照 - Poetic expression meaning 'the setting sun on the mountains and rivers,' evoking a sense of grandeur and timelessness.

  • 未必龙山如此防 - Rhetorical question or statement suggesting that even the majestic Dragon Mountain might not be as formidable as described.
  • 座中三馆尽英才 - Means 'the three halls are filled with talented individuals,' indicating a gathering of distinguished people.
  • 千古雄豪几人在 - Reflective question about how many of the great heroes and warriors from ancient times still exist.
  • 百年怀抱此时开 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the long-held aspirations of a century are realized now,' suggesting fulfillment after a long wait.
  • 连朝倥偬簿书堆 - Describes a busy day filled with official documents, indicating a hectic administrative life.
  • 辜黄花酒一杯 - Phrase that could mean 'wasting a cup of yellow flower wine,' suggesting regret or missed opportunities.
  • 凡作诗和韵为难 - Means 'it is difficult to compose poetry in a given rhyme scheme,' highlighting the challenge of poetic composition.
  • 古人赠答皆以不拘韵字 - Indicates that ancient poets were not bound by strict rhyme schemes in their exchanges.
  • 迨宋蘓黄凡唱和须用元韵 - Refers to the practice in Song Dynasty poets like Su Shi and Huang Tingjian, who used the original rhyme scheme in their exchanges.
  • 往返数回以出竒 - Means 'going back and forth several times to achieve something unusual,' suggesting a process of refinement.
  • 和韵非古要为勉强 - Statement that using a given rhyme scheme is not ancient practice and requires effort.
  • 如能以彼韵就我意何如 - Rhetorical question suggesting that if one can use the given rhyme scheme to express their own thoughts, it would be a remarkable achievement.
  • 绣坼旧图翻短褐 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the old tapestry is torn apart and replaced with a simple robe,' suggesting a transformation from grandeur to simplicity.
  • 朱书小字记归舟 - Means 'red ink and small characters record the returning boat,' indicating a detailed and personal record.
  • 屏山大称其工用事也 - Statement that Ping Shan highly praised the work for its skillful use of allusions.
  • 杨刘变体号西昆 - Refers to the Western Kun style, a poetic style associated with Yang and Liu, two prominent poets.
  • 窃笑登坛子美村 - Phrase that could mean 'secretly laughing at the simple village where the poet Zimei (Zuosi) once stood,' suggesting a contrast between grandeur and simplicity.
  • 大抵俗儒无正眼 - Means 'generally, common scholars lack proper insight,' criticizing the superficial understanding of some scholars.
  • 惟应后世自公言 - Means 'only future generations can speak truly,' suggesting that true understanding comes from later perspectives.
  • 光生杜曲今千丈 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'the glory of Du Fu's poetry shines like a thousand-foot curve,' praising the enduring impact of Du Fu's work.
  • 派出江西本一源 - Means 'the origin of Jiangxi poetry is one source,' suggesting a shared foundation in Jiangxi poetry.
  • 此道陵迟嗟久矣 - Means 'this path has declined for a long time,' expressing regret over the decline of a tradition.
  • 不才安敢擅专门 - Modest statement meaning 'how dare I claim expertise in this field,' expressing humility.
  • 乐府虚传山抹云 - Refers to the *Yuefu* poetry, which is said to have been inspired by the imagery of clouds brushing against the mountains.
  • 诗名浪得栁连村 - Means 'the poet's reputation is falsely gained from the imagery of willows connecting villages,' suggesting that the poet's fame is undeserved.
  • 九原太白有生气 - Means 'in the nine regions, Li Bai (Li Po) still has vitality,' suggesting that Li Bai's spirit lives on.
  • 千古少陵无间言 - Means 'throughout the ages, Du Fu (Du Shaoling) is without reproach,' praising Du Fu's integrity and talent.
  • 登太山巅小天下 - Means 'climbing the summit of Mount Tai makes the world seem small,' suggesting a sense of grandeur and perspective.
  • 到昆仑口知河源 - Means 'reaching the mouth of Kunlun reveals the source of the river,' suggesting a journey to the origin of things.
  • 如君少进可入室 - Means 'if you advance a little more, you can enter the inner sanctum,' suggesting that further progress will lead to mastery.
  • 顾我今衰不及门 - Means 'but I am old and cannot reach the threshold,' expressing regret at one's own limitations.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 夢中作詩或得句多清迈出塵 - Describes the quality of poetry or poetic lines composed in dreams, characterized by a purity and transcendence that rises above the mundane.
  • 山路崭有壁松风清无尘 - A poetic line that uses natural imagery to convey a sense of clarity and purity, with '崭有壁' suggesting a steep and clear path, and '松风清无尘' evoking the freshness of a pine forest breeze.
  • 落月浸天池 - A poetic image that describes the moonlight reflecting on a lake or pool, creating a serene and tranquil scene.
  • 白雁来时泽国秋 - A poetic line that describes the arrival of white geese as a sign of autumn in a marshy region, evoking a sense of seasonal change and the beauty of nature.
  • 如鬼语也 - A phrase that suggests the dream content is mysterious or otherworldly, as if spoken by a ghost.
  • 先翰林罢御史闲居淮阳种五竹堂后自娯作诗 - Describes the actions of a former official who, after retiring from his post, planted five bamboo trees behind his hall in Huaiyang for personal enjoyment and composed poetry.
  • 拨土移根卜日辰森森便有气凌云 - A poetic line that describes the planting of bamboo, with the act of digging up and transplanting the roots, choosing a favorable day, and the resulting growth that seems to reach towards the sky.
  • 真成阙里二三子大胜樊川十万军 - A poetic line that uses a metaphor to compare the bamboo to the disciples of Confucius (from Qufu) and the army of Fan Zhongyan (from Fan川), suggesting the bamboo's elegance and strength.
  • 影浸凉蟾牕上见声敲寒雨枕边闻 - A poetic line that describes the shadow of the moon (凉蟾) seen through the window and the sound of rain heard from the pillow, creating a peaceful and intimate atmosphere.
  • 林间故事传西晋不数山王咏五君 - A poetic line that refers to stories from the Western Jin dynasty, specifically mentioning the 'Five Gentlemen' (五君) and comparing them to the 'Mountain and Water' (山王) of the time, suggesting a connection to the literary and cultural heritage of the era.
  • 以寄赵闲闲防闲闲亦于闲闲堂后种竹甚多 - Describes the act of sending a message or gift to Zhao Xianxian, who also planted many bamboo trees behind his hall, indicating a shared appreciation for bamboo and its symbolic significance.
  • 因出其诗云君家种竹五七个我亦近栽三四竿 - A poetic line that describes the exchange of poems between two individuals, each mentioning the number of bamboo trees they have planted, suggesting a mutual interest in the subject.
  • 两地平分风月破大家留待雪霜看 - A poetic line that describes the division of the wind and moonlight between two places, with the implication that the beauty of the scene will be appreciated even in the harsh conditions of winter.
  • 土膏生意叶犹卷客枕梦魂声已寒 - A poetic line that describes the vitality of the earth and the curling leaves of the bamboo, while also evoking the coldness of a dream and the sound of a guest's pillow, suggesting a contrast between the natural world and human experience.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 先祖龙山君 - Refers to the author's ancestor, a figure of respect and admiration.
  • 先子 - Refers to the author's father or a respected elder, indicating a family tradition of literary and cultural engagement.
  • 阙里二三子 - Refers to the disciples of Confucius, a reference to the cultural and moral authority of Confucianism.
  • 樊川 - Refers to the area around Chang'an, the capital of the Tang dynasty, and is often associated with the poet Du Mu, who is known for his poetry about the natural beauty of the region.
  • 赵闲闲 - Refers to Zhao Xianxian, a person who shares a similar interest in bamboo and poetry, indicating a network of cultural and literary connections.
  • 山王 - Refers to the 'Mountain and Water' (山王) of the Western Jin dynasty, a reference to the literary and cultural figures of that era.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 正大初 - Refers to the beginning of the Zhengda era, a period of the Jin Dynasty (1232–1234). This is a time reference.
  • 先君 - A respectful term for the deceased father of the author, used in classical Chinese to refer to one's late father.
  • 由叶令召入翰林 - Was summoned from the post of magistrate of Ye to the Hanlin Academy. This is a formal expression indicating a promotion or appointment to a prestigious scholarly position.
  • 诸公皆集余家 - All the scholars gathered at my family's residence. This indicates a gathering of literati or officials at the author’s home.
  • 春旱有雨 - A spring drought was followed by rain. This is a poetic way of expressing the arrival of rain after a period of drought.
  • 共赋诗以好雨知时节当春乃发生为韵 - Together composed poems using the rhyme pattern from the line *Good rain knows the season; it falls in spring to bring life*.
  • 赵闲闲得发字 - Zhao Xianxian was assigned the character *fa* (發). This refers to the poetic game where each poet takes a character from a given line to compose their verse.
  • 君家南山有衣鉢 - Your family's southern mountain has robes and bowls. This is a metaphor for a family with a long tradition of learning or monastic life.
  • 丛桂馨香老蟾窟 - The fragrance of clustered osmanthus lingers in the old moon cave. This is a poetic image, likely alluding to a legendary or symbolic place.
  • 从来青紫半门生 - From ancient times, half of those in official robes (青紫) were students of the family. *青紫* refers to the official robes of high-ranking officials.
  • 今日儿孙牀满笏 - Today, the descendants fill the room with official staffs (笏). This is a metaphor for the family's continued success and official status.
  • 云卿复秀出 - Cloud卿 (a title or name) once again stands out as exceptional. This may be a name or honorific.
  • 论事观书眼如月 - In discussing matters and reading books, his eyes are as bright as the moon. A metaphor for wisdom or clarity of thought.
  • 岂惟传家秉赐彪 - Not only is he worthy of passing on the family's legacy and imperial favor (彪). *彪* may be a name or a term for excellence.
  • 亦复生儿勮勔勃 - Also, he has sons named Jue, Mian, and Bo. These are likely the names of the author’s brothers or other family members.
  • 曽乗御史骢 - Once rode a horse of the Censorate (御史). *御史骢* refers to the official horse of a censor, a symbol of office.
  • 霜蹄聊一蹶 - His hooves, white with frost, made a single leap. A metaphor for a brief but significant action.
  • 双凫古邑试牛刀 - Two geese (a metaphor for officials) tested their skills in the ancient town. *双凫* may be a metaphor for officials or a name.
  • 百里政声传马卒 - The reputation of governance in a hundred-mile jurisdiction spread even to the horsemen. This praises the official’s reputation.
  • 视草直金銮 - Serving in the imperial court, drafting imperial edicts. *视草* is a term for drafting imperial decrees, and *金銮* refers to the golden phoenix hall, the imperial palace.
  • 云章妙手看挥发 - His skilled hands produced wonderful imperial documents. *云章* refers to imperial writings or edicts.
  • 老夫当避一头地 - An old man should step aside and let him take the lead. A metaphor for being surpassed by a younger person.
  • 有慙老骥追霜鹘 - Shameful for the old steed to chase the frosty falcon. *老骥* is a metaphor for an old but still capable person, *霜鹘* is a falcon.
  • 健笔纵横建安骨 - His strong and vigorous brush has the spirit of Jian'an literature. *建安骨* refers to the bold and vigorous style of poetry from the Jian'an period.
  • 良防得四并 - Good defense has four alliances. *良防* may be a name or a term for good defense, *四并* may refer to four alliances or four strengths.
  • 更许深杯辞百罚 - He is willing to drink deeply and accept a hundred penalties. *辞百罚* is a term from drinking games.
  • 我辞不饮愿助勇 - I refuse to drink, but I wish to support courage. This is a polite way of declining a drink.
  • 未厌归途洗靴袜 - Not yet tired of returning home to wash one's boots and socks. A humble and practical reference to returning home after a journey.
  • 得好字因用解嘲 - Because he received the character *hao* (好), he used it to mock himself. This is a poetic game where the character is used as a basis for self-mockery.
  • 春寒桑未稠 - In spring, the mulberry trees are sparse. A reference to the season and agricultural conditions.
  • 歳旱麦将槁 - The year is dry, and the wheat is withering. Another reference to drought and its impact.
  • 奚翅万金寳 - How much more than a treasure worth ten thousand gold? *奚翅* is a rhetorical question meaning 'how much more than.'
  • 吾宾适在席 - My guest happened to be at the banquet. *吾宾* refers to the author's guest or companion.
  • 喜气溢襟抱 - Joy filled his chest. A poetic way of saying the guest was very happy.
  • 酒行不计觞 - The drinking went on without counting the cups. A reference to a drinking session without limits.
  • 花底玉山倒 - The jade mountain (a metaphor for drunkenness) collapsed beneath the flowers. A poetic image of intoxication.
  • 从来悭混嘲 - From ancient times, stinginess has been mocked. *悭混* is a term for stinginess or being stingy.
  • 盖为俗子道 - This was said for the sake of the common people. A reference to the speaker addressing the common folk.
  • 北海得开尊 - Běihǎi (a name or title) opened the wine. *北海* may be a name or a reference to a place or person.
  • 天气岂常好 - The weather is not always good. A metaphor for life's unpredictability.
  • 况当生发辰 - Especially on the day of birth. *生发辰* may be a specific day or a poetic way of referring to the day of birth.
  • 霑足恨不早 - The rain was sufficient, but it came too late. A lament for the delayed rain.
  • 东风又吹檐滴干 - The east wind again blew the eaves dry. A poetic image of the rain stopping.
  • 主人不悭天自悭 - The host is not stingy; it is heaven that is stingy. A play on words using *悭* (stingy) in both human and divine contexts.
  • 后月余先君以病不起 - A month later, my father passed away from illness. A sad and direct statement.
  • 赵以天悭为诗谶云 - Zhao took the word *tian qian* (heaven is stingy) as a poetic omen. *诗谶* is a term for a poem that predicts or foreshadows future events.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 正大初 - Zhengda was the era name of Emperor Wanyan Hui of the Jin Dynasty (r. 1232–1234). This is a specific historical reference.
  • 翰林 - The Hanlin Academy, a prestigious scholarly institution in imperial China responsible for drafting imperial edicts and advising the emperor.
  • 叶令 - A magistrate of the county of Ye, likely a small town or county in northern China.
  • 好雨知时节当春乃发生 - This is a well-known line from Du Fu’s poem *春夜喜雨* (Spring Night Rain), used here as a poetic rhyme pattern.
  • 赵闲闲 - A name, likely Zhao Xianxian, a scholar or poet of the time.
  • 衣鉢 - Originally Buddhist terms for robes and bowls, used here as a metaphor for a family with a long tradition of learning or monastic life.

[151]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 元裕之李长源同乡里各有诗名 - Yuan Yuzhi and Li Changyuan are fellow townspeople, each with a poetic reputation.
  • 由其不相下颇不相咸 - Because they did not yield to each other, they were not very friendly.
  • 李好愤怒 - Li was prone to anger.
  • 元尝云长源有愤击经 - Yuan once said that Changyuan had a tendency to express his anger through poetry.
  • 元好滑稽 - Yuan was good at humor.
  • 李辄以诗讥骂元亦无如之何 - Li would often mock Yuan with poetry, and Yuan could do nothing about it.
  • 元尝权国史院编修官 - Yuan once served as an official in charge of compiling the national history.
  • 俄以人言其罪又放出 - Soon, due to public opinion, he was accused of a crime and released.
  • 元因赋金谷怨乐府诗 - Yuan then wrote a poem titled 'Resentment of Jin Gu' in the style of a folk song.
  • 李见之作代金谷佳人答一篇以拒焉 - Li saw it and wrote a reply titled 'Reply to the Beautiful Lady of Jin Gu' to counter it.
  • 一时士人传以为笑谈 - At the time, scholars passed it on as a joke.
  • 元诗云娃儿十八娇可怜亭亭袅袅春风前天上仙人玉为骨人间画工画不出小小油壁车轧轧出东华绣带盘绫结云裾踏雁沙娇云一片不成玉被风吹去落谁家岂无年少恩泽侯锦鞯貂帽亦风流不然典取鹔鹴裘四壁相如堪白头金谷楼台杳无主燕子不飞花着雨只知环佩作离声谁解琵琶得 - Yuan's poem goes: 'The eighteen-year-old girl is charming and pitiful, standing gracefully in the spring breeze. She is a celestial being made of jade, beyond the reach of earthly painters. Her small oil-lacquered carriage creaks as it leaves the East Gate, her embroidered sash and cloud-like skirt trail behind her. A piece of tender cloud, not yet formed into jade, is blown away by the wind, where will it fall? Is there no young nobleman with grace and favor, wearing a brocade saddle and a fur cap, also fashionable? Otherwise, take out a purple silk robe, four walls of Sima Xiangru can turn white-haired. The pavilions and terraces of Jin Gu are now empty, the swallows no longer fly, the flowers are rained on. Only the sound of jade pendants and the parting voice are known, who understands the lute?'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 元裕之 - Yuan Yuzhi, a poet.
  • 李长源 - Li Changyuan, a poet.
  • 末帝 - The last emperor of a dynasty.
  • 布萨阿哈 - Bu Sa A Ha, a former Imperial Son-in-Law.

  • 公既喜遂书不倦 - Since he was pleased, he wrote without tiring.
  • 雷与屏山皆不工书 - Lei and Ping Shan were both not skilled in calligraphy.
  • 赵公尝笑之曰 - Zhao Gong once laughed and said.
  • 希颜堂堂如此而写如此字 - Xiyang looks so dignified, yet writes such characters.
  • 阖坐大噱 - Everyone in the room laughed loudly.
  • 恕先篆不减唐人 - Shuxian's seal script is not inferior to that of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 然迄宋百余年不经诸名士发 - However, for over a hundred years since the Song Dynasty, it had not been praised by famous scholars.
  • 雷希颜趣售之 - Lei Xiyang eagerly sold them.
  • 其鉴裁如此然其书不减李屏山 - His judgment is like this, and his calligraphy is not inferior to Li Ping Shan's.
  • 年月日微雨中为张倩书 - On a certain day in the rain, I wrote for Zhang Qian.

  • 学至于佛则无所学 - A philosophical statement implying that once one reaches Buddhism, there is nothing more to learn.

  • 窃吾佛书者也 - A statement suggesting that these scholars secretly used Buddhist texts.

  • 屏山因以禅语解中庸那着无多事只怕诸儒认识神 - A statement suggesting that Li Pingshan used Zen language to explain the *Zhongyong* (Doctrine of the Mean), implying that there is little to fear from the scholars' understanding of the divine.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 将临月于李家取儿以入 - Complex phrase meaning 'to bring a child from the Li family to the palace during the imperial visitation period.' Refers to the practice of selecting a child from a noble family to be raised as a potential heir.
  • 月日不偶则规别取以为皇嗣 - Phrase meaning 'if the selected child is not auspicious, then another child should be chosen as the heir.' Reflects the superstitious practices of the time regarding the selection of heirs.
  • 先皇平昔或有幸御李氏嫉妬 - Phrase meaning 'the late emperor occasionally had relations with the Li family, and the Li family was jealous.' Suggests a complex relationship between the emperor and the Li family.
  • 令女巫作纸木人鸳鸯符以事魇昧致绝圣嗣 - Phrase meaning 'the Li family ordered a witch to make paper and wooden figures and double-headed fish symbols to cause harm and prevent the continuation of the imperial lineage.' Refers to the use of witchcraft to harm the imperial family.
  • 遣大臣按问俱已款服云云 - Phrase meaning 'the emperor sent high-ranking officials to investigate, and they all confessed as stated.' Indicates the outcome of the investigation.
  • 不着萨克蘓媒孽罪恶之由 - Phrase meaning 'the reason for the slander and accusation of the evil deeds of Sakasu is not mentioned.' Suggests that the source of the slander is not clearly stated.
  • 与此志详畧互异】 - Phrase meaning 'this differs from the detailed records in the annals.' Indicates that the account in the text differs from other historical records.
  • 且废为庶人使天下止呼其小字李师儿 - Phrase meaning 'and he was demoted to commoner status, and the world was only allowed to call him by his nickname, Li Shier.' Refers to the demotion of a person to commoner status.
  • 其母王坐诛兄锡尔弟特尔格皆窜北边 - Phrase meaning 'his mother, Wang, was executed, and his brother Xiel and younger brother Telge were exiled to the northern border.' Describes the consequences for the family.
  • 李氏一门灰灭矣 - Phrase meaning 'the entire Li family was extinguished.' Indicates the complete downfall of the Li family.
  • 当其盛时不减唐开元杨贵妃家 - Phrase meaning 'at its peak, it was no less than the family of Yang Guifei during the Kaiyuan period of the Tang dynasty.' Compares the status of the Li family to that of Yang Guifei's family.
  • 然止于奢纵不能害政蠧民也 - Phrase meaning 'however, it was only about indulgence and did not harm politics or the people.' Suggests that the family's excesses did not have a significant negative impact.
  • 世言李氏姿色不甚丽性慧頴能迎合人主意以此幸于章宗初 - Phrase meaning 'it is said that the Li family was not particularly beautiful, but they were intelligent and could flatter the emperor's intentions, thus being favored by Emperor Zhangzong initially.' Describes the reasons for the family's initial favor.
  • 不知书后见上好文遂能作字知文义 - Phrase meaning 'they did not know how to read or write, but later, seeing that the emperor liked literature, they learned to write and understand the meaning of texts.' Indicates the family's efforts to learn and adapt.
  • 妇人女子变化有此哉 - Phrase meaning 'how can women and girls change like this?' Expresses surprise at the transformation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 改元天興 - Changing the era name to Tianxing; refers to a new reign title or era name adopted by a ruler, often marking a significant political or ideological shift.
  • 勿擊眾 - Do not attack the crowd or do not strike the masses; likely referring to a command to avoid attacking civilian or non-combatant groups.
  • 攻三日不解城 - Attacking for three days without relieving the city; implies that the siege was not successful in breaching the city walls.
  • 城將隳 - The city will collapse or the city is about to fall.
  • 城上望見北兵焚礮車 - The people on the city walls saw northern soldiers burning artillery wagons; a reference to military equipment being destroyed.
  • 眾皆以相賀 - The people all rejoiced among themselves; a collective expression of celebration.
  • 北兵不退駐兵邏之 - The northern troops did not retreat but stationed soldiers to patrol; indicating a prolonged military presence.
  • 知禍未艾 - Realized that the calamity was not yet over; the disaster was ongoing and not yet concluded.
  • 士庶徃徃縱酒肉歌呼 - Scholars and commoners often indulged in drinking and feasting, singing and shouting; a description of decadent behavior.
  • 無久生心 - They did not have long-term thoughts of survival; indicating a lack of concern for the future or survival.
  • 秋七月北兵遣唐慶等來使 - In the seventh month of autumn, the northern troops sent Tang Qing and others as envoys; a diplomatic mission.
  • 欲和好成金主當自来好議之 - Wishing to make peace, the Jin emperor should come himself to negotiate; the Jin emperor (likely Emperor Wanyan Liang) was expected to personally attend peace talks.
  • 末帝托疾臥御榻上 - The last emperor pretended to be ill and lay on the imperial bed; a sign of avoidance or refusal to meet the envoys.
  • 見慶等掉臂上殿不為禮 - He saw Tang Qing and others casually walking into the hall and did not offer them proper courtesies; a display of disrespect.
  • 致來防畢仍有不遜言 - After the envoys had finished their audience, there were still disrespectful remarks; indicating continued insolence.
  • 近侍皆切齒 - The attendants were all filled with hatred; a strong emotional reaction.
  • 飛虎軍數輩 - A few members of the Feihu Army; a military unit known for its strength.
  • 不若殺之快眾心 - It would be better to kill them, which would please the people; a justification for assassination.
  • 夜中持兵入館大噪殺慶等 - At night, they took weapons and entered the guesthouse, making a great noise and killing Tang Qing and others; a violent and sudden attack.
  • 館伴使鄂屯安濟庫二人 - Two guesthouse attendants, Eton Anjiku; the names of the envoys' hosts.
  • 遲明宰執趨赴館視之 - At dawn, the ministers hurried to the guesthouse to see what had happened; a response to the assassination.
  • 軍士露刃詣馬前請罪 - Soldiers showed their weapons and approached the horse to ask for punishment; a display of accountability or submission.
  • 宰相惶遽慰勞之上因赦其罪 - The prime minister was frightened and consoled them; the emperor then pardoned their crime; a political resolution to the incident.
  • 識者知其禍不 - Those with insight knew that the disaster was not yet over; a final warning or realization.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 改元天興 - Changing the era name to Tianxing refers to a historical event in the Jin dynasty. The era name 'Tianxing' was used by Emperor Wanyan Liang of the Jin dynasty, who ruled from 1161 to 1189.
  • 北兵 - Northern troops refers to the Jin dynasty forces, which were the northern power opposing the Southern Song dynasty.
  • 金主 - Jin emperor refers to the ruler of the Jin dynasty, likely Emperor Wanyan Liang.
  • 末帝 - The last emperor refers to the Southern Song emperor, likely Emperor Duzong (Zhao Kuo), who ruled from 1189 to 1207.
  • 飛虎軍 - Feihu Army was a famous military unit of the Southern Song dynasty, known for its discipline and combat effectiveness.
  • 鄂屯安濟庫 - Eton Anjiku is the name of the two guesthouse attendants (館伴使) who were killed. Note: This name may be a variant of '奥屯按出虎' (Aotun Anchu Hu), as noted in the text's case note.
  • 京師細民 - The common people of the capital refers to the civilian population of the Southern Song capital, likely Hangzhou.
  • 識者 - Those with insight refers to wise or experienced individuals who could foresee the consequences of the events described.

  • 鄂屯安濟庫 - Eton Anjiku is the name of the two guesthouse attendants. Note: This is a variant of '奥屯按出虎' (Aotun Anchu Hu), as noted in the text's case note.
